title: How NOT to inject the BRAIN! Dr Tim injects temples, cheeks & tear troughs [Aesthetics Mastery Show]
filename: How NOT to inject the BRAIN! Dr Tim injects temples, cheeks & tear troughs [Aesthetics Mastery Show].en.vtt
excerpt: Welcome to the ascetics mastery show have we got a special lockdown treat for you guys which is we are sharing some never seen before footage from dr tim live last year where i go through a full face treatment we did a 14 mil treatment we cover treatment design the anatomy inject…

segments.text: welcome to the ascetics mastery show have we got a special lockdown treat for you guys which is we are sharing some never seen before footage from dr tim live last year where i go through a full face treatment we did a 14 mil treatment we cover treatment design the anatomy injection technique for a full face so tons of stuff to learn not used to having an assistant so i keep doing things myself i have an unprimed needle why would i not prime the needle anyone gathered that before anyone gathered that before aspiration that's right so i'm looking for every little incremental increase in safety i can get and voluma aspirates well i've tested it you may have seen the video of me testing it works really well it's a good product to aspirate with even when there's filler in the syringe but if i have no filler in the syringe it's going to be even more sensitive because i'm just trying to pull back the air and the chances of me getting a little

fleck of blood because just the tip of the needle's in the wrong place it goes up and that's going to make me over the course of my career a little bit safer and every little bit counts anyone heard about a case where someone doing temples injected someone's brain with filler yeah so what can you do the question then comes it's only your personality in the frontal lobe so you still be able to do most things so what can you do to reduce the risk of that so these things are continuously pop into my head the angle of your needle would change that risk completely because if i angle in such a way that i'm aiming towards where the orbital bone is then i'm if i go too deep i'm going to keep going into the orbit so all that means is the difference between an angle i don't know if you can see it yeah that's great an angle like that versus an angle like that then changes where i'm going to go if i go too deep so i'm always looking for little things that chip away at risk

and make my patients just a little bit safer so that's one more risk ruled out okay so i'm doing one last check as i always do um for any pulsation and i don't feel anything you can see that angle fairly well it's slightly more superior than it looks on the screen because of the angle i'm gonna pop in and i'm actually at a point in the temple which is furthest away from where those vessels should be aspirate give it a second and then i can start delivering my product now the next thing i'm doing at this point is watching my patient for a response a lot of the worst occlusions i've heard of have been really painful during the injection so anyone who's suffering i would stop find another point now i'm putting a little bit of pressure anterior to that so that i don't let too much product flow backwards

nice and slow now temples are a risk because of the site the volume of the injection that's why we do the extra steps which i recommend like not priming your needle for example i recommend you do that anywhere where you're injecting a large bolus nice and slow letting it flow in i'm actually not putting much pressure on at all now i remember from her picture that she had zero temple before so i know i can completely correct this and i'm also remember buying myself the ability to do a bit more work on her cheeks without the aesthetic this today

if it flows too easily and it's not very um it doesn't project very well like low lift or any softer products it just it may not actually do the lifting we want so when you get a bleed like that we need to hold it for at least a minute having a peak a little bit too soon always backside always backfires so we'll leave that for at least a minute um i know from experiences i usually do half to one mil at a time and there are occasionally you have um i've been on these expert panels where they decide like what's safe and they throw numbers around like how much did you put in one area my experience of that is that there really isn't any anything other than thought around that like there aren't any cases of people putting two meals in and causing pressure that blocks an artery or and people with one meal never having that problem it's just not known so i think what happens is there's a lot of them there's a lot of um of kind of where your way the kind of realm that

you play the volume that you're used to putting in is what you gravitate towards um i nearly always use a needle because i can place it deeper on the periosteum you i do use cannula sometimes the slight risk is kind of bogginess it's quite thin skin so you can end up a little bit boggy um which some patients complain about but it's low risk so unprimed needle feel for the for any pulsation so it's the left side sometimes a little bit more painful that he's got a good pain threshold there so once again angled so i'm going into the little v shape by the bone and angle towards the bone so if i go too deep i'm not going to be in the wrong place aspirate that's negative and then start delivery so i don't want to move my tip again once i've started injecting

i don't want to move it again i'm monitoring lindsay's response to see if she reacts differently do an answer to the cannula question i have done that multiple times but it's i wouldn't try and correct the whole temple with a cannula currently with the way i currently see it because of the bogginess but i might do a little bit over the top um to kind of smooth something in to get the final bit of the result out that would as you get more into this you do more and more complex versions more and more layering because

okay so how does temple affect eyebrows you can sometimes get lift from eyebrows it's that stretching effect which we talked about before you can sometimes get um that's looking better you can sometimes relax orbicularis oculi through expanding the tissue here and it just releases some of those fibers and you get a little bit of a lift in some people okay so i can really see this is less of a risk now with her cheek this is flowing more gently and i can go on to the next phase so i'm going to use the luma now with a let's go for a long 25 gauge canyon so i'm going to now attempt to lay the first layer of support for her cheek so i'm replacing that lateral fat pad i'm going to put a candler in and do a little bit just on top here just a first pass and i'll probably adjust that again later on pilot hole i'm trying to layer aim it in the direction my candle is going to go and i only need

to go through the dermis i don't need to go through everything and that's enough for me i'm now in the hypodermis in the fat so getting your candler in i see all sorts of ways of getting it in as in there's a this kind of stirring thing with a needle where you put the needle in then go round and round my idea of what's going on there is your the sharpest bit the needle is doing something underneath and then the blunt bit that's not really that good at stretching the skin it's just going round around so it's not necessarily that effective and mostly i don't think you need to do very much at all other than just aim properly just pop a little um swab on top of that so anyone who's seen me you do this before i think i'm unusually gentle compared with many i really don't like the idea of tearing through to get somewhere i like to try and find an easy route so i'm just trying lots of different angles maintaining my overall direction of aim and trying to sneak through without causing any trauma and we're right back

now so now i'm right where i want to be i'm on the superior surface of the zygoma and this is where particularly with a cannula it's very sculptural i'm just kind of little it's a lot like shaping it you know if you're a sculptor and you're chipping away a bone but it's the reverse because additive but i'm just adding little bits i'm seeing what the result is like i'm checking it doing a bit more and just gradually increasing the volume this is volume as well so i'm also having asked that question about the temple i am actually slightly in the temple now because i can see a little shadow from where i'm at i don't know if you can see it where i can just see the connection between the cheek and the temple and i just think i'm going to smooth that slightly but going back to how we started i have an aesthetic goal which is i want this to project out a little but not too much for the temple and i'm continuously referencing that

goal as i'm going i use the what i can see in terms of visibility of the cannula to adjust to decide where the product goes as the cannula bulges i think yeah that's what i want to achieve so that's the electrical projection let me do a little bit of smoothing so the next thing i'm going to do which is harder for you to see is just is do some anterior cheek projection i just know from the if you turn your head a little bit to the side you might see that this area expected more further forward than up here so i'm basically trying to get the cheek to project a little bit more like that and that's part of what will help lift the lower face as well so this i prefer to do um deep and it's onto the anterior surface of the zygoma you can feel the angle of the zygoma here so what i'm now seeing in my mind's eye is this alignment with the zygoma but i'm on the

anterior surface of looking at the skeleton you might be able to picture that the anterior surface of the zygoma i'm trying to augment now the artery as we said it runs medial to it the facial artery and then beneath it or inferior to it as the transverse facial arteries this is a relatively safe place to go deep and touch the bone aspirate okay and then a little bit of molding just to shape that in so now we'll do the same on the other side

your supporting hand is very important for cannulas in terms of feeling where the product is going adjusting the tissue as you're sliding in finding that easy route and i get painting off in the way i layer that layer the product i'm just trying to layer evenly and sculpt that shape it's um it's adhered so it's underneath the structures but it's not most cannula procedures are in the hypodermis you can be in deep hypodermis or superficial hypodermis and it's actually why quite often i do quite like using needles to get a little bit deeper but it's pretty hard with the cannula

even your best interest best attempt to go very deep so where is it good to be deep um particularly where you might have um a little bit of puffiness occurring so usually in the tear trough we want to be a little bit deeper and i'm going to do a little bit in the lateral legit junction just to blend the cheek in with a couple of injections of bow left and these i want to be on the periosteum so much safer in terms of the puffiness that you might get it's a better place to have them so lateral legit junction is a really important part of periorbital restoration um a lot of i see i still see people treating the tear trough as if it's a different entity and you see the medial aspect of the tear trough improved and then they leave the lateral aspect that's um it's down to a training gap because the lateral logic junction is usually more aging than the titrator in my opinion so i'm just trying to get there's a little line i can see i don't know how however you can see it on the screen but i just want to get that little line

to project up that little shadow there is really important for um overall restoration so once again i'm aiming in such a way that if i go too deep i'm not going to hit an eye an eyeball it's kind of crazy one of the cases of blindness temporary blindness thankfully was uh on the teuter of course where they missed went on the medial border of the bone and then injected the i think criminal output so if i angle in such a way that if i go too deep there's no way i'm going to hit anything other than bone that's exactly what i want to achieve so i'm going to go also an unprimed needle is pretty superficial .05 might be my first pass now i'm going to change angle trying not to bruise by going through the same hole and going

up okay now remembering what i noticed about the slight downturn to her eye i might attempt to just give a little bit of a lift to the lateral part of her eye and but it's this you can't see it so well in here maybe on this side you can see it that little there's a little tiny shadow just there that if i can get that to look slightly more like that it will give her a more positive aura instantly for a very small amount of product i can't see it i think the light is hiding it but i can see it from where i am so we'll do a little tiny amount with that so i'm looking for little superficial veins i'm going to enter i'm on the periosteum absolutely must touch bone around here and i'll do a little squirt 0.05 and then shape that so first pass i often use a finger and

then i use the cotton bud it's just easier to flatten something in a crude way with your finger and you get the sensory feedback from using your finger and then the cotton bud just allows me to roll it in a way that fingers are a little bit less good so once again you're looking for those little veins to avoid this is a bit of a bruising area touching periosteum now some people shape in here and it can look like you're expecting to hit the orbital rim and actually you don't and you can go in and hit the eye so i'm always very slow as i enter from there because it's quite rare but i've had them where you feel in fact lindsay has a tiny hint of one there's like a little dip in the skull and i've had a couple of patients where that dip is quite basically pronounced into a v shape so if you feel above and below you expect that to be bone but actually underneath there isn't so that's very important to know about it's not that common but does happen so i'm just going to do a light tear trough treatment just to add a little bit of volume and try and subtract that shadow

just a tiny bit now going back to remember the slide of all the complexity and the kind of the way when you start to look and think that there's lots of other things that are still popping into my head but i'm trying to do the foundations first and then i might come back and do some tweaking to try and add even more simplicity as i was saying before that case of the infra orbital artery being occluded could have been caused simply by an angle of entry that was just slightly more inferior like that so i'm always trying to enter now um at right angles to the facial plane

i'm also aiming immediately because most of the vessels are a bit more lateral and then where i touch the bone i should be not in an artery but we check as always a good long aspiration have a good look and then we do it all by us average bolus here is probably 0.2 you can go to 0.3 in some people quite easily and a little bit of shape you can do this with a cannula too i'm quite happy with the anatomy that it's the that it's actually not it shouldn't really be near the artery with all those things in place but cannula is acceptable you will be in the fat though it's very hard to be as deep without causing quite a lot of pain

okay my shaping is just to create that the pressure is slightly medial i don't want that to be a bump and then a shadow i need this to blend in harmoniously so i'm just looking at this i'm looking at the shape of overall shape of a cheek and i don't know if you can see it on the screen but when you elevate that up you create more of that catch light that we saw in the cartoon i want i don't want this to be a have a reflection and then a little shadow and then a reflection i want that to be projected anteriorly so i'm going to add a bit with voluma it's medial to the midfielder line so i tend to use a cannula here because it's safer with the vessels we've got two ligaments to think about here you've got the zygomatic retaining ligament and orbicularis oculi retaining ligament i want to be underneath the zygomatic the orbicularis oculi retaining ligament in the little v shape and that means i pass through the zygomatic ligament once just into the dermis through the dermis

into the hypodermis so i am going to be close to some vessels here but a cannula that's right angle to the vessel is and that with someone who's being gentle is incredibly likely unlikely to cause an occlusion or to even get into the vessel in such a way i have tried on the cadaver course to get into a facial artery with a cannula and it is possible that i'm pushing harder than i've ever pushed on a real person so one of the factors i think it's hard to explain in terms of overall risk is the analog components like we can all debate about needles versus cannulas but how do you do how do you discuss gentle versus rough it's very hard to even prove what either one of those are one of our for one of us gentle might be one thing for others it looks quite rough so but i

think there's something around just being almost treading on eggshells with candles that will make you safer the downside with inclusion with a cannula is that you have this far bigger injury normally when it does happen you hardly ever get into a vessel but when you do it's far bigger so there are certain places where i would never put a candle into a bleeding hole mainly the nose because you might be parallel with the vessels it might actually be the vessel you're going into but i'm going at right angles and to what i think is that's a facial vein that's bleeding if i feel a bit of resistance that makes me this and i certainly wouldn't if i pop

through anything that feels significant then i might stop and aspirate again although i don't actually think in places where the cannula is very mobile that it's really likely at all that you'll be in a vessel but it does work you can get positive aspirations with certainly with the more easily flowing materials you can get a positive so it's worth doing if you're unsure so i'm looking for the tip of this canyon using that as my guide feeling with my finger as well and then i'm constantly in my mind trying to think of this shape i'm trying to create for her cheek i'm layering it on referencing that and then i do a little bit molding the molding afterwards is a really important part of the whole thing i don't think you can create a perfect shape just with injections because product tends to flow where it flows like if there's a low pressure point in the skin it just goes there so now i'm just going to do a little trickle in the theater off very low volumes with a cannula

and try and be as deep as i can but it will be more superficial than with a needle but i'm not gonna put very much in this is volbella which is one i've used for the longest and a product i feel really happy with in terms of tear trough okay so i'm looking for that little tiny track where i can see the camera is i do it nearly all by movement i can't see it when i'm still and i'm looking for this mobility as one of the safety aspects i don't want to see a tip that doesn't move and i also hang on the uh on seeing a little bolus as i inject to tell me that i'm not injecting into a vessel i don't know if you've ever noticed that injecting botox occasionally you'll get into a vein and it just the product just disappears instantly because it's you haven't actually you haven't you're not in a um in the dermis or the hypodermis you're in a vessel so it gets washed away

and that is a sign to stop injecting i don't like injecting filler if i don't get some feedbacks on your feedback okay so look at that see what the calendar is i'm trying to be relatively deep but i need it's there's very little room i'm actually feels like i'm quite close to the bone but i still see the shape of it quite easily little painting strips i do little strips it's much more like painting than filling with the soft product once again noticing those little bulges as i inject that's reassuring very small amounts i can see the shape of it which is normal at this stage in total that's point two so point one each side very little let us know in the comments
what you think was the most useful thing or if you've got any questions and we will answer them during lockdown so thanks very much for watching see you next week
